# Category Theory Cheatsheet {.appendix}

A quick reference for the categorical concepts that appear in this tutorial. Each entry gives a one-sentence definition, where it shows up in panproto, and a pointer to the literature.

## Category

A collection of objects and morphisms (arrows between objects) equipped with composition and identities, satisfying associativity and unit laws.

**In panproto**: The category **GAT** has theories as objects and theory morphisms as arrows. The category **Mod(T)** has models of a theory $T$ as objects and model homomorphisms as arrows. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 1].

## Functor

A structure-preserving map between categories: it sends objects to objects and morphisms to morphisms, preserving composition and identities.

**In panproto**: A theory morphism $F: T_1 \to T_2$ induces a functor $F^*: \mathbf{Mod}(T_2) \to \mathbf{Mod}(T_1)$ that pulls back models. This is the `restrict` operation—data migration in the reverse direction. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 1].

## Natural Transformation

A family of morphisms between two functors that commutes with the functors' action on morphisms.

**In panproto**: Natural transformations between migration functors correspond to systematic ways of transforming one migration strategy into another. They ensure that different migration paths yield compatible results. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 2].

## Limit

A universal cone over a diagram—the "most general" object that maps into every object in the diagram compatibly.

**In panproto**: Products (a special case of limits) appear when combining independent schemas. The limit construction ensures no information is lost. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 5].

## Colimit

A universal cocone under a diagram—the "most general" object that every object in the diagram maps into, gluing shared parts together.

**In panproto**: Colimits of theories are how new protocols are defined. The pushout (a colimit of a span) merges two theories along a shared base. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 5] and [@adamek1994, Ch. 11].

## Pushout

The colimit of a span $A \leftarrow S \rightarrow B$: the universal object that glues $A$ and $B$ together along their shared sub-object $S$.

**In panproto**: `colimit(t1, t2, shared)` computes a pushout of theories. This is the primary mechanism for composing component theories into protocols. See [@barr1990, Ch. 9].

## Adjunction

A pair of functors $F \dashv G$ between two categories such that $\mathrm{Hom}(FA, B) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(A, GB)$ naturally. The left adjoint $F$ freely constructs; the right adjoint $G$ forgets structure.

**In panproto**: The migration functors $\Sigma_F \dashv \Delta_F \dashv \Pi_F$ form adjoint triples. $\Delta_F$ (restrict) is the pullback; $\Sigma_F$ (left push) and $\Pi_F$ (right push) are its adjoints. These adjunctions guarantee the round-trip properties of lenses. See [@spivak2012] and [@maclane1998, Ch. 4].

## Kan Extension

The universal way to extend a functor along another functor. Left and right Kan extensions generalize colimits and limits, respectively.

**In panproto**: The left and right pushforward operations ($\Sigma_F$ and $\Pi_F$) are computed as left and right Kan extensions along the theory morphism $F$. See [@maclane1998, Ch. 10] and [@spivak2012].

## Fibration

A functor $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ with a lifting property: for every morphism in the base and object in the fiber above its codomain, there is a canonical "cartesian" lift.

**In panproto**: The projection from the category of all schemas (across all protocols) down to the category of theories is a fibration. Lifting along this fibration is what lets panproto migrate schemas when the underlying protocol theory changes. See [@barr1990, Ch. 12].

## Initial Algebra

The least fixed point of an endofunctor: an algebra $F(A) \to A$ such that every other $F$-algebra receives a unique homomorphism from it.

**In panproto**: W-types are initial algebras of polynomial functors. Recursive data structures (trees, nested records, lists) are modeled as W-types, and their migrations are derived from the universal property of initiality. See [@adamek1994, Ch. 10] and [@gambino2004].

## Polynomial Functor

An endofunctor built from sums, products, and exponentiation by a fixed set: $P(X) = \sum_{a:A} X^{B(a)}$. Polynomial functors classify branching structures.

**In panproto**: Each schema determines a polynomial functor whose initial algebra is the type of well-founded data conforming to that schema. Migrations between schemas lift to maps between the corresponding polynomial functors. See [@gambino2004] and [@abbott2005].
